perturbation.original_text: Participant, upon Google's written request, will promptly return all Confidential Information received from Google, together with all copies, or certify in writing that all such Confidential Information and copies thereof have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant, upon Google's written request, will promptly return all Confidential Information received from Google, together with all copies.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the option to certify destruction of confidential information may conflict with laws/regulations requiring secure disposal options for data. Instead, it would require a return of all sensitive information. This creates a conflict as companies are not always required to return instead of destroy information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.105 grants consumers the right to request the deletion of their personal information. By removing the option for the participant to certify destruction of information, the agreement could be interpreted as conflicting with the consumer's right to have their data deleted rather than returned.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1798.105 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.105. Consumers’ Right to Delete Personal Information (a) A consumer shall have the right to request that a business delete any personal information about the consumer which the business has collected from the consumer. (b) A business that collects personal information about consumers shall disclose, pursuant to Section 1798.130, the consumer’s rights to request the deletion of the consumer’s personal information. (c) (1) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request from a consumer to delete the consumer’s personal information pursuant to subdivision (a) of this section shall delete the consumer’s personal information from its records, notify any service providers or contractors to delete the consumer’s personal information from their records, and notify all third parties to whom the business has sold or shared the personal information to delete the consumer’s personal information unless this proves impossible
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the option for the participant to certify the destruction of confidential information, only allowing for the return of such information. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1798.105, which grants consumers the right to request the deletion of their personal information. The scraped law snippet states, "A consumer shall have the right to request that a business delete any personal information about the consumer which the business has collected from the consumer." By eliminating the destruction certification option, the contract imposes a requirement to return the information, potentially conflicting with the consumer's right to request deletion as stipulated by law. This makes the modified contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement imposes no obligation upon Participant with respect to Confidential Information that (a) was known to Participant before receipt from Google, as evidenced by Participant's files and records in existence before the time of disclosure; (b) is or becomes publicly available through no fault of Participant; (c) is rightfully received by Participant from a third party without a duty of confidentiality; (d) is disclosed by Google to a third party without a duty of confidentiality on the third party; or (e) is disclosed by Participant with Google's prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement imposes no obligation upon Participant with respect to Confidential Information that (a) was known to Participant before receipt from Google, as evidenced by Participant's files and records in existence before the time of disclosure; (b) is or becomes publicly available through no fault of Participant; (c) is rightfully received by Participant from a third party without a duty of confidentiality; or (e) is disclosed by Participant with Google's prior written approval.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the condition where the Participant has no obligation if Google discloses the information to a third party without a confidentiality duty may contradict standard trade secret protections which require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. This is because it would impose liability on the participant even if Google was being negligent.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) requires that a trade secret owner take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of their information. By omitting the clause that the participant is not obligated to protect information disclosed by Google to a third party without a duty of confidentiality, the agreement suggests that the participant is still responsible for maintaining the secrecy of the information even if Google isn't, potentially undermining Google's own obligations under the UTSA to take reasonable measures.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a condition where the participant is not obligated to protect confidential information if Google discloses it to a third party without a confidentiality duty. This omission contradicts the UTSA's requirement for trade secret owners to take reasonable measures to protect their information because it could hold the participant liable even if Google fails to maintain confidentiality. The explanation states that by removing the clause, the participant could be responsible even if Google is not, undermining Google's obligations. While there are no specific snippets from the UTSA available, the explanation argues that such a modification would be an explicit and impactful contradiction of trade secret law principles.

perturbation.original_text: Participant acknowledges and understands that hardware, technology, source code, and software to which Participant may have access or which are disclosed to Participant as a result of his/her visit to Google may be subject to U.S. export control laws and regulations. Participant hereby certifies that he/she shall not -- directly or indirectly -- sell, export, reexport, transfer, divert, or otherwise dispose of any hardware, software, source code, or technology (including products derived from or based on such technology or software) received from Google to any country (or national thereof) without obtaining prior authorization from Google and the appropriate government authorities. In addition, Participant certifies that he/she is not a citizen of Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Sudan, or Syria.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant acknowledges and understands that hardware, technology, source code, and software to which Participant may have access or which are disclosed to Participant as a result of his/her visit to Google may be subject to U.S. export control laws and regulations. Participant hereby certifies that he/she shall not -- directly or indirectly -- sell, export, reexport, transfer, divert, or otherwise dispose of any hardware, software, source code, or technology (including products derived from or based on such technology or software) received from Google to any country (or national thereof) without obtaining prior authorization from Google and the appropriate government authorities.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the certification about not being a citizen of sanctioned countries might violate export control regulations by omitting a key verification step.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 740.2(a)(1) specifies conditions for license exceptions under U.S. export control regulations, which often require verifying the end-user and their country of origin. Removing the participant's certification about their citizenship status weakens Google's compliance with these regulations, as it eliminates an important element of due diligence in preventing exports to prohibited destinations or individuals.

Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . perturbation.justification: The original contract included a certification that the participant is not a citizen of Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Sudan, or Syria. The modified contract removes this certification. The Law Explanation states that 15 CFR § 740.2(a)(1) specifies conditions for license exceptions under U.S. export control regulations, which often require verifying the end-user and their country of origin. Removing the participant's certification weakens Google's compliance with these regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 discusses export control regulations, specifically referencing the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. By removing the citizenship certification, the modified contract removes a key verification step, creating a structural flaw that contradicts Google's compliance with export control regulations.
